schools.summary: Gill: 'the apostles and brethren in Judea' (the twelve and the members of the churches, now more than one) heard of Cornelius's conversion; the momentous event — the Gentiles (the uncircumcised, wholly outside the covenant), not Jews or Samaritans only, receiving the word, the middle wall beginning to fall (Ephesians 2:14); the news exciting interest and objection (the Jewish prejudice making it controversial), the providence bringing the question of the Gentiles' admission before the church.

themes: the Gentiles had also received the word of God

schools.summary: Barnes: the circumcision party contending with Peter (disputing, reproving, charging him with fault) shows conclusively that the early church did not regard Peter as having any supremacy — they would not have freely reproved the head of the church (a witness against later claims of his primacy); the objection over his going to the uncircumcised (the deep Jewish prejudice against fellowship with Gentiles), the matter settled by evidence not decree, the dispute drawing out the truth's confirmation.

debates.question: Does the contention with Peter bear on his supposed supremacy?

sides.position: Yes — the free reproof shows the early church did not regard Peter as supreme or infallible

themes: they that were of the circumcision contended with him

schools.summary: Clarke: in a Jew, eating with the uncircumcised was no small offense — held a positive transgression of the law and custom of separation, and the circumcision party (not knowing Peter's reason) reasonably called him to account; Gill: Peter went into their houses, lodged, conversed, ate with them; the depth of the Jewish prejudice (the separation a defilement in their eyes), the barrier the gospel had to break to embrace the Gentiles, the breach of the old separation the opening of the new fellowship (what God had cleansed, v.9).

themes: thou wentest in to men uncircumcised

schools.summary: Barnes: Peter rehearsed the matter from the beginning in order — a simple, unvarnished statement of facts the best way to disarm prejudice and silence opposition (which arises from prejudice or false report, removed not by angry contention but by a plain relation of facts); the meekness of Peter (an apostle answering the reproof humbly, not standing on authority — a pattern against his supposed supremacy); the right way to meet opposition (calm, orderly truth, not heat or pride), vindicated by its success (v.18).

themes: Peter rehearsed the matter from the beginning

schools.summary: Ellicott/Robertson: Peter's retelling adds touches of vivid personal recollection (the sheet coming 'even to me,' a detail not in Acts 10:11) — the eyewitness recalling the scene; Wesley: the trance suspends the outward senses; Peter begins his account with the vision at Joppa (granted in prayer) — the great sheet from heaven, the divine preparation for the Gentile mission; grounding the whole proceeding in God's revelation (not his own choice), the divine origin the warrant for the mission.

themes: a vision, a great sheet let down from heaven
the divine origin of the mission

schools.summary: Ellicott/Robertson: the vividness — Peter recalling the intense, eager gaze on the vision; Gill: the eyes of the mind (a mental vision); the sheet containing all creatures, clean and unclean together (the forbidden and the allowed), figuring the abolition of the distinction of clean and unclean (Leviticus 11) and, more deeply, the admission of the Gentiles (whom the Jews counted unclean) — the ceremonial law fulfilled in Christ (Romans 10:12), preparing Peter to count no man common (Acts 10:28).

themes: fourfooted beasts, creeping things, and fowls
the abolition of the old distinction

schools.summary: Gill: Peter apprehended the heavenly voice in his mind (as Paul in his trance); Robertson discusses the construction; the voice commanding Peter to 'slay and eat' of all creatures (including the forbidden unclean) — declaring the old distinction of clean and unclean abolished (the food-law set aside in Christ, 1 Timothy 4:4), testing Peter's obedience against his lifelong scruples; the deeper meaning — figuring the command to receive the Gentiles (count no man common, Acts 10:28), the divine authority abolishing the separation.

themes: the command to disregard the distinction

schools.summary: Gill: Peter took the voice to be the Lord's yet was not obedient (even though hungry) — showing how strict an observer of the ceremonies he was, how deeply prejudiced in their favour; 'Not so, Lord' the refusal, never having eaten the common or unclean; the depth of the ingrained distinction (so deep even a divine command met resistance, repeated three times, v.10); the difficulty of overcoming long prejudice (conscientiousness mixed with prejudice), his reluctance showing the new liberty was God's command, not his invention.

themes: nothing common or unclean hath entered my mouth
Peter's deep attachment to the law

schools.summary: Gill: the heavenly voice answered again, correcting Peter's refusal; the great principle 'What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common' — God has cleansed what the law called unclean (all meats, Titus 1:15), abolishing the distinction; the deeper meaning — the Gentiles cleansed (by the gospel, not the law), no longer common or unclean (Acts 10:28), received by God; the cleansing God's act (final and authoritative), the human prejudice to yield, the believer to receive what God receives.

themes: what God hath cleansed, that call not thou common

schools.summary: Gill: the voice answered three times, encouraging Peter; Ellicott/Robertson mark the vividness of 'drawn up' (a rapid upward movement, personal recollection); the threefold repetition confirming the certainty and importance of the lesson (as Pharaoh's doubled dream, Genesis 41:32, 'established by God') and overcoming Peter's reluctance (his refusal met by patient, emphatic repetition until his prejudice yielded); the sheet withdrawn, the lesson delivered, providentially joined to the messengers' arrival (v.11).

themes: all were drawn up into heaven

schools.summary: Gill: the moment the vision ended, the three men from Cornelius were at the house — the vision and the messengers providentially joined; God ordering the exact timing so the vision (no man common) and its application (a call to a Gentile) coincided, the vision interpreted by the arrival; the providence guiding the great step (leaving nothing to chance), and serving Peter's defense (the mission of God, not his contrivance) — God joining instruction to its occasion.

Clarke: the six brethren probably present as witnesses to confirm Peter's account; Ellicott/Robertson: 'nothing doubting' (or 'making no distinction') the same word as 'contended' (v.2) — Peter, guided by the Spirit, raised no debate such as the objectors were raising; the Spirit's direct command authorizing his going (not his choice), obedience without doubting; the six witnesses confirming the facts (the truth in many witnesses, 2 Corinthians 13:1), answering the objectors' doubt.

themes: the Spirit bade me go, nothing doubting

schools.summary: Gill: 'in his house' fitly added (since an angel entered the house, Peter and the brethren could not be criminal in following), the angel called both man and angel (appearing in human form); Cornelius's angel directing him to send for Peter — the divine call on the Gentile's side matching Peter's vision, God working at both ends (preparing the apostle to go and the seeker to send); the Gentile mission divinely initiated at both ends, serving Peter's defense (the providence the argument).

themes: he had seen an angel in his house
the divine call on the Gentile's side

schools.summary: Gill: 'words' = doctrines of Christ's person and office, peace, pardon, righteousness, salvation (the gospel Peter preached); Barnes: 'all thy house' also baptized and saved (Cornelius feared God with all his house, Acts 10:2); salvation by the preached word (the angel directing to a preacher, not preaching himself — faith by hearing, Romans 10:17; the dignity of preaching, 1 Corinthians 1:21), the promise embracing the whole household, the gospel blessing the family.

themes: words whereby thou shalt be saved

schools.summary: Gill: 'began to speak' (Luke's idiom for the thing itself) — it was toward the close of the discourse the Spirit fell; the Holy Ghost falling on the Gentiles 'as on us at the beginning' (Pentecost), the same gift and sign (tongues, Acts 10:46); God's decisive seal on the reception of the Gentiles (given before baptism and without laying on of hands, that none might doubt it was God's act), the Gentile Pentecost matching the Jewish — the divine settling of the question, Peter's strongest argument (v.17).

themes: the Holy Ghost fell on them
as on us at the beginning

schools.summary: Clarke: Christ's words (Acts 1:5) supposed by many to refer to the apostles alone, but Peter here believed them a promise to all Christians, Jew or Gentile, who believe — seeing the Spirit fall on the Gentiles as a fulfillment; the baptism with the Holy Ghost surpassing John's water (the inward gift surpassing the outward sign), the promise catholic (for all the church, not apostles or Jews only); the remembered word interpreting the event, the Scripture and the event illuminating each other.

debates.question: Was Christ's promise of Spirit-baptism (Acts 1:5) for the apostles only or all believers?

sides.position: For all believers, Jew and Gentile — Peter saw its fulfillment in the Gentiles

themes: John baptized with water; ye with the Holy Ghost
remembered the word of the Lord

schools.summary: Barnes: what power or right had Peter to oppose the manifest will of God that the Gentiles be received? — God had shown his intention by giving them the Spirit, overcoming Peter's prejudices, to resist being to fight against God; the argument grounded in God's own act ('the like gift,' no difference made); the duty of submission to God's manifest will (Romans 9:19), the humility of 'what was I?' (counting himself nothing against God), the conclusive argument silencing the objectors (v.18).

themes: God gave them the like gift
what was I, that I could withstand God

schools.summary: Barnes: the objectors 'held their peace and glorified God,' convinced by the manifest will of God; the great truth established — the doors of the church opened to the entire Gentile world, changing the apostles' views, breaking their prejudices, teaching them to look on all men as brethren; the confession 'God hath granted the Gentiles repentance unto life' — repentance God's gift (Acts 5:31; 2 Timothy 2:25), 'unto life' (saving, issuing in eternal life); the prejudice overcome, the Gentile inclusion owned and glorified.

themes: they held their peace, and glorified God
God hath granted the Gentiles repentance unto life
the door opened to the Gentiles

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the persecution no slight benefit — all things work together for good (Romans 8:28); had they studied how best to establish the church they could have done no better than the persecution dispersing the teachers, spreading the gospel; the scattered believers carrying the word to Phenice, Cyprus, Antioch, but 'to none but the Jews only' (the old limitation, the Gentile mission a distinct act of God, not the natural development); the order — to the Jew first (Romans 1:16); the gospel reaching Antioch, the future center of the Gentile mission.

themes: scattered upon the persecution about Stephen
as far as Phenice, Cyprus, and Antioch
at first to the Jews only

schools.summary: Barnes: the men of Cyprus and Cyrene, Greek-speaking, led to address the 'Grecians' (Hellenists — whether Greek-speaking Jews or, more likely, Gentiles) at Antioch, 'preaching the Lord Jesus'; the bold step beyond preaching to Jews only (v.19), the gospel reaching the Greek-speaking world (parallel to and confirming Cornelius's reception); the founding of the church at Antioch, the future center of the Gentile mission (sending out Paul and Barnabas, Acts 13); the Christ-centered preaching, the same gospel to Greeks as to Jews.

themes: the gospel to the Greek world

schools.summary: Gill: 'the hand of the Lord' not only providence, love, wisdom, but above all the hand of power (the arm of the Lord, efficacious grace) which when revealed makes the gospel believed — but without it no work is done, ministers labour in vain; the power of God attending the preaching at Antioch, so 'a great number believed, and turned unto the Lord' (faith and turning, true conversion); conversion the work of God's power (1 Corinthians 3:7, God giving the increase), the minister depending on him.

themes: the hand of the Lord was with them
a great number believed, and turned

schools.summary: Barnes: the Jerusalem church, hearing of the conversion of the Gentiles and the success at Antioch, sent Barnabas to aid the disciples and give their sanction (as they sent Peter and John to Samaria, Acts 8:14); Barnabas, a native of Cyprus (Acts 4:36), peculiarly qualified; the care of the mother church for the new work (confirming, aiding, overseeing, maintaining unity), the providence supplying the right leader (a good man, full of the Holy Ghost and faith, v.24, who would bring Saul, v.25).

themes: tidings came to the church at Jerusalem
the church's care for the new work

schools.summary: Gill: 'the grace of God' Barnabas saw = the evident effects of efficacious grace in the converts (regeneration, conversion, enlightening, quickening so many souls); Barnabas 'was glad' (the gracious man rejoicing at God's work, without envy or party spirit); his exhortation 'with purpose of heart cleave unto the Lord' (settled resolve, steadfast commitment, not wavering, cf. Joshua 23:8); the encourager (Acts 4:36) exhorting new converts to perseverance, the pastoral care of establishing them.

themes: had seen the grace of God, was glad
with purpose of heart cleave unto the Lord
joy at the grace of God

schools.summary: Gill: Barnabas 'a good man' (grace in his soul, doing good works, kind, generous, charitable, having sold his land, Acts 4:37); the threefold commendation — a good man, full of the Holy Ghost (the Spirit's power and gifts), and of faith (strong trust); the 'for' connecting his character to the fruit (because he was good and Spirit-filled, much people were added); the qualifications of a fruitful minister (goodness, the Spirit's fullness, faith), character and fruit joined, God blessing such a ministry.

themes: a good man, full of the Holy Ghost and faith
much people was added unto the Lord

schools.summary: Barnes: the great success and revival at Antioch called for additional labour (as harvest-time seeks aid), Saul in the vicinity (Acts 9:30), eminently fitted, and known to Barnabas (Acts 9:27); the generosity and wisdom of Barnabas — seeking out a greater man than himself to share the fruitful work (no jealousy or self-seeking), the providence joining the two for the great Gentile mission (Acts 13); the self-effacing love preferring the work's good above his own honour (Romans 12:10).

themes: Barnabas departed to Tarsus to seek Saul

schools.summary: Barnes: Antioch an important city, the apostles attending chiefly to cities as centres of influence; the whole year of teaching establishing the church (the future base of the Gentile mission); 'the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch' — the name derived from Christ, variously held to be given by enemies/heathen in derision, taken by the disciples, or providentially given; whatever its origin, the honoured title of Christ's people (named after him, 1 Peter 4:16), fitting where Jew and Greek were united in one church.

sides.position: Given by enemies/heathen in derision (a nickname for Christ's followers)
Taken by the disciples or providentially given — a fit name marking them as Christ's

themes: a whole year they taught much people
the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch

schools.summary: Gill: Christian prophets came from Jerusalem to Antioch — men with the gift not only of expounding the mysterious OT prophecies but of foretelling things to come; the reality of the prophetic gift in the apostolic church (a gift of the Spirit, 1 Corinthians 12:10; 14:1, including interpretation and prediction); the prophets edifying the church and (Agabus, v.28) foretelling events; the link between the mother church and the new (the prophets a bond of unity), the providence sending them to warn and prepare for the famine.

themes: came prophets from Jerusalem unto Antioch
the prophets of the apostolic church

schools.summary: Gill: Agabus an itinerant prophet, signifying by the Spirit a great dearth 'throughout all the world' (the Roman oikoumenē), fulfilled under Claudius (historically attested famines); the prophecy by the Spirit and verified by the event — the reality of the Spirit's foreknowledge and the genuineness of the prophetic gift attested by fulfilment; the providence in the warning (foretold that the church might prepare relief, vv.29-30), the foreknowledge serving practical love and the church's charity.

themes: great dearth throughout all the world
fulfilled in the days of Claudius

schools.summary: Barnes: 'according to his ability' = as each had prospered (not that they were rich, but each as he could afford, 1 Corinthians 16:2); the Antioch disciples, warned by the prophecy, preparing relief beforehand (the foresight of love); the unity and mutual care of the church (the new Gentile church aiding the Jewish mother church), the Gentile converts particularly bound to aid the Jews from whom they received spiritual things (Romans 15:25-27, the spiritual debt repaid in temporal aid); charity uniting Jew and Gentile.

themes: every man according to his ability
determined to send relief to Judaea

schools.summary: Gill: the disciples not only determined but acted, performing the charity (the resolve carried out, James 4:17); Barnes: the first mention in the NT of 'elders' (presbyters) in the Christian church, the relief sent to the elders (the officers who received and distributed it), carried by Barnabas and Saul; the order and government of the church shown in the elders (the appointed channel for administration and charity); the charity entrusted to faithful hands, integrity and order in handling the church's relief.

themes: sent it to the elders by Barnabas and Saul
